title: The Imbalanced Data Challenge
concept: Imbalanced datasets occur when one category is extremely rare (like 0.1% fraud) compared to the other (99.9% normal). A model that always guesses 'normal' gets 99.9% accuracy but is useless.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes data preparation so important in machine learning?

options.text: Clean, well-formatted data helps models learn reliable patterns rather than garbage.
It guarantees the model will run twice as fast on any machine.
It automatically changes a regression model into a classification model.

exercises.explain: Correct! Preparing data ensures models learn from clean, high-quality signals instead of noise.

cards.title: Spotting a needle in a haystack
cards.body: Imagine sorting apples where 9,990 are red and only 10 are green. If your robot helper is lazy and decides to label EVERY apple as "red," it will be correct 99.9% of the time. But did it actually learn to find the green apples? No!

cards.title: What is Imbalanced Data?
cards.body: Imbalanced data is a dataset where one category (the majority class) is vastly more common than the other (the minority class). This makes it easy for models to look smart by simply guessing the common class every time.

cards.title: The Always-No Weather App
cards.body: Imagine a weather app in a desert town where it only rains 1 day a year. If the app is designed to always predict "No Rain," it will be 99.7% accurate. But it will fail to warn you on the one day you actually need an umbrella!

cards.title: Credit Card Fraud
cards.body: In real-world credit card transactions, fraud is extremely rare. For every 1,000 transactions, maybe only 1 is fraudulent (0.1%). A model could reach 99.9% accuracy just by saying "Approved" to everything—while letting thieves steal millions.

cards.title: High accuracy means a great model
cards.body: You might think a model with 99% accuracy is ready to launch. In reality, on imbalanced data, high accuracy is often a trick. It just means the model is excellent at ignoring the rare events you actually care about.

cards.title: Majority vs. Minority Class
cards.body: How the distribution differs based on rare outcomes:

left.text:
The common category.
e.g., 99.9% normal transactions
or healthy patients.

right.text:
The rare category.
e.g., 0.1% fraud transactions
or rare diseases.

exercises.prompt: Why is a credit card fraud detector that always guesses 'safe' useless, even if it gets 99.9% accuracy?

options.text: It fails to detect the actual fraud cases.
It requires too much electricity to run.
It deletes all the normal transactions.
It changes the transactions to a regression problem.

exercises.explain: Accuracy is misleading when the target is rare. The model must actually spot the fraud to be useful!

exercises.prompt: True or false: In an imbalanced dataset, the 'minority class' is the category that has the most examples.
exercises.explain: False. The minority class is the rare category with very few examples.

exercises.sentence: A dataset is ___ when one category has vastly more examples than the other.
exercises.explain: Imbalanced data occurs when there is an uneven distribution of categories.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario describes an imbalanced dataset challenge?

options.text: A medical dataset has 9,999 healthy patient scans and only 1 scan showing a rare tumor.
A dataset contains an equal number of dog and cat pictures.
A model predicts the temperature in degrees Fahrenheit.

exercises.explain: The medical dataset is highly imbalanced because the tumor scans are extremely rare, making it hard for the model to learn to spot them.

exercises.prompt: You are training an AI to detect spelling errors. In your test data, 99.5% of the words are spelled correctly, and 0.5% have typos. If your model gets 99.5% accuracy but fails to find a single typo, what is the best explanation?

options.text: The model is just guessing the majority class ('correct spelling') because it is so common.
The dataset needs to be converted into continuous numbers first.
Spelling correction is a regression task and cannot use accuracy.
The typo data has too many feature columns.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The model learned that it can get 99.5% accuracy by simply lazy-labeling every word as correct. This is the classic imbalanced data trap.
